ai_systems.findings:
New evaluation frameworks are reshaping how we measure multi-agent research systems. ScienceAgentBench, developed by OSU and Princeton, introduces 102 rigorously validated tasks drawn from 44 peer-reviewed publications across bioinformatics, computational chemistry, GIS, and psychology. Each task requires agents to produce executable Python programs, with expert validation ensuring scientific authenticity. The current leaderboard shows o3 Medium achieving 33.33% accuracy at $11.69 cost per task, while Claude Sonnet 4.5 reaches 30.39% at $7.47, highlighting the accuracy-cost trade-off in autonomous scientific discovery.

Meanwhile, evaluation methodology itself is evolving. A new framework from USC addresses fundamental challenges in benchmarking scientific multi-agent systems: distinguishing reasoning from retrieval, preventing data contamination, establishing ground truth for novel problems, and managing continuously updating knowledge bases. The authors propose contamination-resistan [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Three fresh 2026 sources reveal an active congressional UAP transparency push. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna’s House Oversight Task Force is pressing the Department of War for withheld UAP videos after a September 2025 hearing, citing national-security risks. Dr. Eric Davis told IBTimes he has already briefed multiple congressional committees in SCIFs about UFO crash retrievals and Non-Human Intelligence programs, constrained only by presidential NDAs. Meanwhile, USA Today notes three rounds of open UAP hearings since 2023, with Obama’s recent “aliens are real” remark reigniting public interest even as he walked it back.

weather: Today in Edmonds: Mostly cloudy becoming mostly sunny, high 63°F. Light north wind 5-10 mph. Just a 10% chance of light rain after 5 PM. Tonight turns partly cloudy with low around 51°F and calm northeast breeze. Pleasant spring day—comfortable for walks with Piper, no jacket needed midday but bring a light layer for evening.

local_news.findings: The Washington State Court of Appeals has denied a motion to reconsider its March 16 dismissal of the Rimmer v. City of Edmonds case, allowing the earlier dismissal to stand. This ends the latest chapter in the long-running dispute over a dogwood tree that began when the city denied Nathan Rimmer's permit in May 2022. The Pacific Legal Foundation, representing Rimmer, plans to ask the Washington Supreme Court to provide clarity on whether property owners can recover damages when cities withhold permits to force unconstitutional conditions.

South County Fire Commissioners held a public hearing on a proposed $420 million fire and EMS construction bond that could go to voters in November. The measure would replace or renovate most of the district's aging fire stations and add four new ones to meet growing demand and seismic safety standards. Edmonds residents expressed concerns about the scale and timing, with some suggesting smaller bond amounts over time rather than one large request. Commissioners acknowledged the feedback and plan to continue discussions before making a final decision.

The City of Edmonds issued an update on ongoing efforts to resolve operational issues at the Edmonds Wastewater Treatment Plant. The city approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to assist with operating the troubled carbon recovery system, which has experienced significant delays and technical failures since construction began in 2021. The project cost has escalated from $26 million to $31 million, and the city has spent over $5 million hauling wastewater solids to Oregon landfills while the system remains non-operational.

classroom_ready.title: Token Economy Implementation Guide for 10th Grade ELA
classroom_ready.summary: Complete step-by-step guide for implementing a token economy system in ELA classrooms. Includes effect size data (0.90), behavior contract templates, reinforcer menus, and gradual fading protocols. Features specific examples like plastic chips, punch cards, and privilege-based rewards.

classroom_ready.title: Hero Economy Launch - Classroom Credit System
classroom_ready.summary: Ready-to-print lesson kit with Hero Bank Tracker worksheets, Prize Power Menu posters, Reward Pass coupons, and Master Ledger trackers. Focuses on non-tangible rewards and positive reinforcement through a classroom credit system.

classroom_ready.title: Classroom Cash Economy System
classroom_ready.summary: Complete classroom economy setup with editable templates for classroom cash, reward charts, and behavior tracking. Includes school mascot customization options and practical implementation steps for positive reinforcement.

classroom_ready.title: Token Economy Lesson Plan with Behavior Contracts
classroom_ready.summary: Comprehensive lesson plan for launching a token economy system including behavior contracts, reinforcer selection guides, and implementation protocols. Features free downloadable resources and step-by-step classroom setup instructions.

pedagogy_pieces.title: 10 Ways to Learn with Shake Up Learning This Summer
pedagogy_pieces.summary: Kasey Bell's summer learning guide emphasizing AI-powered classroom management tools, communication templates, and behavior tracking systems. Focuses on practical EdTech solutions to reduce teacher workload while improving student engagement.

english_lesson_plans.notes: All resources are freely available and classroom-ready. The token economy systems emphasize positive reinforcement over punishment, with built-in gradual fading protocols. Each system includes customization options for school mascots and specific classroom needs.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has completed their 2025-26 season auditions, but singers interested in joining for the 2026-27 season can email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to be added to their auditions mailing list. Auditions for all voice parts are held each August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Avenue, Seattle). Requirements include music reading skills, vocalizing, sight-reading two short choral pieces, and preparing a short solo piece. Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30-9:30pm. Northwest Chorale, a 45-member non-auditioned community choir, is performing spring concerts on May 9 at Edmonds United Methodist Church and May 16 at Luther Memorial Church in Seattle. They rehearse Mondays at 7:00pm during the academic year.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries offers multiple toddler storytime options this week: Toddler Storytime with Nathalie at Lynnwood Library on Monday, May 11 at 9:00am, and Toddler Storytime with Heather on Tuesday, May 12 at 9:00am. Edmonds Bookshop is hosting a special story time event featuring children's author and illustrator Jennifer K. Mann. Lynnwood Library also has a creative "Happy Flower Flor Feliz" craft session on Friday, May 15 from 2:00-4:00pm where toddlers can create colorful flowers using recycled materials.

intellectual_journals.findings: In the May 2026 issue, paleontologist Steve Brusatte explores why birds were the only dinosaurs to survive the asteroid impact that ended the Cretaceous period 66 million years ago. While the popular myth suggests all dinosaurs perished, new fossil evidence reveals that modern-style birds survived due to a combination of circumstances: their smaller size, ability to fly, and crucially, their diet of seeds that remained available during the prolonged darkness and cold that followed the impact. The article traces how these avian dinosaurs, already widespread across the globe, possessed the exact [TRUNCATED]

In a May Day essay, Jacobin argues that International Workers' Day remains essential as an antidote to the "politics of isolationism" that dominates contemporary society. The piece critiques how modern culture - from Andrew Tate's "manosphere" to "tradwife" influencers and positive psychology gurus - places responsibility for societal problems on individuals rather than recognizing collective solutions. The essay positions May Day as a counter-narrative that acknowledges "we exist as individuals as part of a society" and that common challenges require common solutions, making the celebration o [TRUNCATED]

A separate May 2026 Scientific American feature examines how SpaceX's pivot toward AI investments - including a potential $60 billion acquisition of AI-code-writing startup Cursor - could derail NASA's Artemis moon program. The article explores tensions between SpaceX's commercial AI ambitions and its contractual obligations to deliver the Starship-based Human Landing System for Artemis IV in 2028. While SpaceX pursues AI-driven data centers in space and other ventures, delays in Starship development raise questions about whether the company's shifting priorities might compromise America's ret [TRUNCATED]

In a deeply reported investigation, ProPublica reveals how the Trump administration's mass immigration arrests are systematically failing under legal scrutiny. The piece documents over 300 cases where protesters and bystanders were arrested during immigration sweeps, only to have charges collapse when video evidence contradicted officer statements. The investigation exposes a pattern where federal agents routinely accuse dissenters of domestic terrorism or extremism, yet more than a third of cases are quickly dismissed, refused prosecution, or lost at trial - suggesting the arrests serve more  [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Reverend Billy Takes On Norman Foster’s New Monolith
newyorker.dek: Fresh from opening shows for Neil Young, the street preacher Billy Talen has moved on from burning Mickey Mouse in effigy to protesting JPMorgan Chase’s ties to fossil fuels, Jake Offenhartz writes.
newyorker.text:
A little after midnight on Earth Day, the street preacher Billy Talen dragged his pulpit to the new Norman Foster-designed global headquarters of JPMorgan Chase, on Madison. His self-ordained mission: deliver a twenty-four-hour sermon, calling attention to the bank’s ties to the fossil-fuel industry. Talen, who is seventy-five, held an umbrella above his white leisure suit and silver bouffant, eying a pair of bank security guards. “Blessings upon your souls, gentlemen,” he bellowed. Members of his activist ensemble, the Stop Shopping Choir, were setting up a live stream in the rain. Savitri Durkee, who directs both the choir and Talen, who is her husband, clipped a mike to his lapel and checked her watch. “It’s go-time, Billy,” she said. “See you on the other side.”
Talen, who goes by Reverend Billy, is fond of what he calls “extreme preaching.” He began in the mid-nineties, marching around Times Square with “Antichrist” Mickey Mouse nailed to a wooden cross, in protest of the neighborhood’s creeping corporatism. In the years since, his focus has shifted to the climate crisis. Often, this involves staging musical protests in hostile venues—banks, pipeline-construction zones—until he and his flock are arrested for trespassing. But, Talen said, even that has begun to feel “predictable.” He elaborated, speaking into a camera: “The only way we’re going to start an effective movement is if each of us goes to the limit of our capabilities and claims our eccentricities. I’m a preacher, so I’m going to preach until I drop.” A choir member checked the stream, and noted that they’d just jumped from five to fourteen viewers. “Earthalujah!” Talen cried.
After decades of sneaking up on bankers and lobbyists, Reverend Billy and the choir recently booked a straight gig: touring the country as Neil Young’s opening act. (Typical song: “A Tree Is the Opposite of a Cop.”) The rock star’s fans seemed receptive to the message, relatively. “After a lifetime of being too strange, people are starting to get us,” Talen said. He’s also the religion editor for the Times-Contrarian, Young’s anti-corporate “digital newspaper.” “Neil is a hands-on publisher,” Talen said. “Sometimes he lets me write these really long things. Other times, he’ll say to me, ‘Billy, what do you mean here? What do you want to leave us with?,’ and we’ll e-mail back and forth on that.” Talen said that he’s learned a lot from Young as a performer: “He’s taught me to hold the rhythm, hit the rhyme. He gets what appeals to a mass audience.” Talen and Durkee were connected with the singer by Young’s wife, Daryl Hannah, whom they met at a 2009 protest against mountaintop-removal coal mining in West Virginia. Talen was later arrested, after depositing mounds of “sacred dirt”—taken from the mountain, he claimed—at two Chase branches in the East Village. The bank has since decreased financing for companies involved in mountaintop-removal mining.
By 8 A.M., dark circles had formed under Talen’s steely blue eyes. “I just need to stay hydrated,” he said. Behind him, a gardener was washing a living-wall installation titled “A Parallel Nature,” designed for Chase by Maya Lin. “I’m finding myself mesmerized by this strange, sculpted nature wall,” Talen said. “It feels like a memory.” After conferring with a guard, the gardener pointed his hose at the protesters in the plaza. Talen pulled a rain slicker over his suit and glowered. Thousands of workers were now streaming into the building, mostly ignoring the doomsday preacher.
Around lunchtime, roughly two dozen choir members and other activists arrived for a 5K run around the bank. Among them was Ahmed, an asylum seeker from Guinea who had taken refuge in the choir’s former headquarters, a vacant bank on Avenue C, after arriving in the United States. “It’s very scary-looking,” he said of Chase’s sixty-story tower. Many of the runners had procured thrift-store blazers or top hats, an effort to blend in with the bankers. Durkee, who wore a pin-striped suit, led the group in some stretches, followed by twelve laps around the block. A construction worker sanding down a railing offered support—“You got this!”—but appeared confused by a runner’s sign accusing Chase of ecocide. “I thought it was, like, a charity thing,” he said.
As night fell, Talen huddled under a blanket with other choir members. His once thunderous voice had dropped to a rasp as he condemned the “gangsters in suits” responsible for rising global temperatures. In the final minutes of his sermon, he found himself at an unusual loss for words. “It feels like, well, extreme exhaustion, I guess,” he said. “Or a long hike that got out of hand, and now I have to come down the mountain.” Had the suffering been worth it? “I’m not sure we cracked the culture,” he said. “But we had a vision, and we did what we could.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
literary_pick.author: Junot Díaz
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2008 Pulitzer Prize and National Book Critics Circle Award, this novel follows Oscar de León, an overweight Dominican-American nerd from New Jersey obsessed with science fiction and fantasy, as he navigates love, family curses (the fukú), and the legacy of Trujillo's dictatorship. Blending Spanglish, comic book references, and footnotes, Díaz creates a multigenerational saga that spans from the Dominican Republic under Trujillo to contemporary New Jersey, exploring themes of diaspora, masculinity, and the weight of history. Critics hailed it as a landmark of 21st-century American [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: May Day: An Answer to Rampant Individualism
enriched_articles.text:
By Esther Lynch

Countless influencers and quack positive-thinking gurus tell us that success is all a matter of individual effort. International Workers' Day reminds us that individuals are happiest when we have others standing alongside us.

Trade unions defend not just workers' conditions but the right to an existence not cramped by the pressures of the job. On May Day, we celebrate workers' right to work less.

enriched_articles.title: Taya Necklace: an $89 AI pendant that refuses to listen to anyone but you
enriched_articles.text: An ex-Apple design engineer just opened public preorders for an AI necklace that flips the always-on wearable script. Taya's mic stays off until you tap it. Even when it's recording, directional mics plus speaker verification only transcribe your voice — bystanders get filtered out at the hardware level.
